title: Evaluating LLM Systems: Check your understanding

questions.question: You update your classification system prompt and the eval score drops from 91% to 74%. What should you do?
options: Deploy anyway: a 74% accuracy is still acceptable for most use cases
Remove the failing examples from the eval set so the score recovers
Block the change, investigate which examples regressed, and fix the prompt before merging
Switch to a larger model to recover the accuracy loss
questions.explanation: A regression in eval score means the change made things worse on known inputs. The eval set is the ground truth: removing failing examples to make the score look better defeats the purpose. Block the change, identify which inputs now fail, understand why, and fix the prompt. Only then re-run and merge.

questions.question: What is the main risk of building your eval set exclusively from examples you used while writing the prompt?
options: The eval set will be too large and slow to run in CI
The model will memorise the examples and overfit to them
The prompt was likely optimised for those inputs, so the eval measures prompt-fitting rather than generalisation
Eval sets must come from production traffic to be valid
questions.explanation: If you wrote the prompt while looking at the same examples you use to evaluate it, the eval measures how well you optimised for those specific cases: not how the system generalises. Use held-out examples the prompt author has not seen. Include edge cases and adversarial inputs the prompt was not designed for.

questions.question: You use an LLM as a judge to score model outputs. Which of the following is a known bias you need to mitigate?
options: The judge model always prefers outputs from the same model family
The judge model tends to favour longer responses regardless of quality
The judge model refuses to evaluate outputs that contain code
The judge model scores lower when the temperature is above 0.5
questions.explanation: Verbosity bias, favouring longer responses, is one of the most consistently documented biases in LLM-as-judge evaluations. Other known biases include positional bias (favouring whichever response appears first) and self-preference. Mitigate by randomising response order and averaging scores across multiple judge calls.

questions.question: Your eval passes at 88% before deploying a new prompt. One week after launch, users report degraded quality. What most likely went wrong?
options: The model provider silently changed the model between deploy and launch
The eval set did not include inputs representative of real user traffic
88% accuracy is too low a threshold: it should have been set to 100%
Eval scores degrade over time as the model forgets the training data
questions.explanation: Pre-deploy evals only catch failure modes present in the eval set. If real user inputs differ from your eval inputs, different phrasing, different topics, different languages, the eval gives a false sense of security. Seed your eval set with real traffic samples, and add any production bug to the eval set immediately so it can't recur.

questions.question: Which metric should you track alongside accuracy when evaluating a prompt change for production?
options: The number of tokens in the system prompt
The model's training data cutoff date
Per-request cost and latency, since accuracy alone does not capture the full production impact
The number of examples in the eval set
questions.explanation: A prompt change that improves accuracy by 2% but doubles token consumption and response latency may not be worth deploying. Evaluate changes across all three dimensions: quality, cost, and latency. An eval that only measures accuracy gives you an incomplete picture of production impact.
